settings.title: How to Water a New Shrub Without Drowning It
settings.excerpt: Most shrubs we replace did not die of thirst, they died of daily light sprinkles that never reached the roots.
settings.body:
The question we hear most after a planting job is how often to water. The honest answer surprises people: less often than you think, but much more deeply.

A daily splash wets the top inch and evaporates by noon. The roots stay dry and grow shallow, which makes the plant weaker the following summer. A shrub trained to expect a daily sprinkle never bothers to send roots down to find its own water, so it relies on you forever.

Water slow and deep

Let a hose trickle at the base for ten minutes, twice a week, in the first season. Then check the soil with your finger before the next watering. If it is damp two knuckles down, leave it. The point is to soak the whole root ball, not just darken the surface.

  • Water early morning or evening, never in midday sun
  • Aim at the base, not the leaves
  • Push a finger in before you reach for the hose

Mulch a hand's depth around the base, but keep it off the stem. Bark piled against the trunk traps moisture where you least want it and can rot the bark. A clear gap of a couple of inches around the stem is all it takes. That single habit holds moisture and cuts your watering in half.

Most shrubs we are called back to replace did not die of thirst. They died of kindness, drowned slowly by daily attention that never reached where it mattered. Trust the finger test over the calendar and you will lose far fewer plants.

If you are unsure how your particular soil drains, tell us when we plant. Heavy clay and free-draining sand want very different routines, and we are happy to write yours down for you.
